SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper describes concrete algorithms (interactive definition induction, Algorithm 2 for chart extension via alignment, and heuristics for alignment) and an implementation in SEMPRE, and reports system-level outcomes (240 structures, 74.7% use of naturalized language). However, the provided text lacks quantitative experimental details, statistical analysis, or comparisons that would fully validate claims; details of training/evaluation protocols and failure modes are absent. The technical components appear plausible but some assumptions (e.g., conservative alignment suffices to avoid low-precision rules) are asserted rather than rigorously validated in the excerpt.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The core idea—letting users provide definitions to incrementally naturalize a core programming language and transplanting derivations via utterance–body alignments—appears novel relative to cited interactive learning and semantic parsing work (e.g., Wang et al. 2016, Zettlemoyer & Collins). The combination of interactive definition induction with alignment-based chart extension is a non-trivial extension beyond existing GENLEX-like and weakly supervised approaches. The paper situates itself against prior work and articulates how definitions extend scope beyond prior interactive denotational supervision.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally organized (motivation, interactive learning procedure, Algorithm 2, heuristics, implementation and discussion) and uses examples (‘‘add palm tree’’) and figures to illustrate ideas. However, the provided excerpt contains repeated sentences and some presentation artifacts which make parts harder to follow, and some components (precise formal definitions, full algorithmic pseudocode and parameter settings) are only sketched rather than fully specified in the text shown.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The work includes a deployed user-facing evaluation (community built 240 structures; usage statistics over 10K utterances), demonstrating real-world application. Nonetheless, the excerpt lacks important experimental analyses: no ablation studies of alignment vs. no-alignment, sensitivity analyses for the heuristics, explicit baselines, or detailed reproducibility information (hyperparameters, dataset, user study protocol). Failure cases and limitations are only briefly noted (precision concerns) without thorough empirical examination.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important and practical problem—bridging programming languages and natural language for complex action spaces—and demonstrates a community-scale interactive deployment, suggesting practical usefulness. The approach could influence future work on interactive language learning and semantic interfaces and opens directions for user-driven language extension in other domains. It is unlikely to be transformative across all NLP/ML but constitutes a strong, broadly relevant contribution.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic—interactive semantic parsing / natural language interfaces to a programmable environment—fits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML conferences. The methods draw on semantic parsing, probabilistic models, and interactive learning; the evaluation includes a user deployment which is appropriate for the venue. The paper is positioned relative to core prior work in the community (SEMPRE, Zettlemoyer & Collins, Wang et al.).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper cites relevant prior work (Wang et al. 2016; Zettlemoyer & Collins; others) and explains conceptually how its use of definitions differs from prior interactive denotational supervision. However, the excerpt does not present empirical comparisons against those baselines or ablations to quantify improvement, so experimental comparisons appear limited or absent in the provided text.
